title: The bookbinder and the flooded Quran
summary: In a workshop near the Medina of Tunis, a bookbinder is repairing a 19th-century Quran damaged in a winter flood.
body:
The workshop sits in a narrow lane behind the Zitouna Mosque in Tunis. Hatem Mejri has worked there for forty years. He bound his first book — a school dictionary, rebound for a teacher — when he was sixteen. His father bound books in the same room, and his grandfather before that. The room smells of old paper, beeswax, and the glue Hatem mixes from rabbit-skin scraps and water.

In February of last year, a Quran arrived in a cardboard box. It had been kept for over a century in a small private library on the coast, inherited from a great-grandfather. A winter storm had pushed seawater into the house. The library had not flooded badly — perhaps two centimetres for half a day — but the Quran had been on a low shelf. The lower third of every page was stained, swollen, and beginning to stick together. The family asked Hatem if anything could be done.

Hatem said yes, although he warned them honestly. He could separate the pages. He could dry them slowly, in a way that would not crack the ink. He could repair the binding and rebuild the cover. He could not, he said, make the water marks disappear. Salt and time had already written themselves into the paper. The book would carry the memory of the flood. After a quiet half hour, the family asked him to begin.

The work has taken nine months so far. Hatem has separated each page by hand, using a thin blade and a brush of warm water applied one drop at a time. He has pressed pages between sheets of cotton and replaced the cotton every two days. Where the paper had thinned, he has reinforced it from behind with a near-transparent Japanese tissue, glued on with a paste so dilute it leaves no shine. The text, written in iron-gall ink, has held remarkably well — although a few words on three pages have faded and required tiny retouching, which Hatem did only after the family agreed and a local scholar advised on the missing letters.

The binding is the final stage. Hatem will sew the pages back together using linen thread, exactly as the original binder did in the nineteenth century, and rebuild the cover with leather from a tannery he has used since 1989. He will not, however, use new leather for the spine. He has set aside the original spine, salt-damaged but still readable, and will incorporate it into the new binding so that the book's older self remains physically present.

The family will collect the Quran next month. They have already decided where it will sit when it comes home — not back on the low shelf, but on a wall stand in the room where the great-grandfather used to read. The water marks, Hatem told them, will fade slightly over the next ten years but never vanish. The family did not seem disappointed. They said the book had now lived through one more thing.

annotations.meaningVi: phồng / căng lên do ngấm nước

annotations.phrase: Salt and time had already written themselves
annotations.meaningVi: muối và thời gian đã 'tự ghi' vào giấy

grammarNotes.title: Past perfect for events before another past event
grammarNotes.bodyVi: B1 dùng past perfect (had + V3) để chỉ một hành động xảy ra trước một thời điểm/hành động khác trong quá khứ. Trong bài: 'It had been kept for over a century in a small private library on the coast...' (giữ trước thời điểm sách đến xưởng). 'A winter storm had pushed seawater into the house.' (xảy ra trước khi gia đình mang sách đến). 'The library had not flooded badly... but the Quran had been on a low shelf.' (đều ở trước thời điểm họ nhận ra hư hỏng). 'Salt and time had already written themselves into the paper.' (đã ghi vào giấy trước lúc Hatem bắt đầu). So sánh với 'The work has taken nine months so far' (present perfect — kéo dài đến hiện tại).
grammarNotes.bodyEn: B1 uses past perfect (had + V3) for an action that happened before another past action. In the text: 'It had been kept for over a century...' (before the book reached the workshop). 'A winter storm had pushed seawater into the house.' (before the family brought the book in). 'The library had not flooded badly... but the Quran had been on a low shelf.' (both before they noticed the damage). 'Salt and time had already written themselves into the paper.' (before Hatem began). Compare 'The work has taken nine months so far' (present perfect — extending to now).

translationVi:
Xưởng nằm trong một ngõ hẹp phía sau Nhà thờ Zitouna ở Tunis. Hatem Mejri đã làm việc ở đó bốn mươi năm. Ông đóng cuốn sách đầu tiên — một cuốn từ điển học sinh đóng lại cho một giáo viên — khi mười sáu tuổi. Cha ông đóng sách trong cùng căn phòng, ông nội trước đó. Phòng nồng mùi giấy cũ, sáp ong, và loại keo Hatem vẫn tự pha từ vụn da thỏ và nước.

Tháng Hai năm ngoái, một cuốn Quran đến trong một thùng các-tông. Nó đã được giữ hơn một thế kỷ trong một thư viện tư nhỏ ven biển, thuộc về một gia đình thừa kế từ cụ cố. Một cơn bão mùa đông đã đẩy nước biển vào nhà. Thư viện không ngập nặng — có lẽ hai centimet nước phủ sàn trong nửa ngày — nhưng cuốn Quran ở kệ thấp. Một phần ba dưới mỗi trang đều ố, phồng, và bắt đầu dính vào nhau. Gia đình hỏi Hatem có thể làm gì không.

Hatem nói có, dù ông cảnh báo thật lòng. Ông có thể tách các trang. Ông có thể hong khô chúng, từ từ, theo cách không làm nứt mực. Ông có thể sửa phần đóng và làm lại bìa. Nhưng ông không thể, ông nói, làm vết nước biến mất. Muối và thời gian đã tự ghi vào giấy. Khi xong, cuốn sách sẽ mang theo ký ức của trận lụt. Sau nửa giờ im lặng trong xưởng, gia đình xin ông bắt đầu.

Đến giờ công việc đã chín tháng. Hatem tách từng trang bằng tay, dùng một lưỡi dao mỏng và một cọ nước ấm nhỏ từng giọt. Ông ép trang giữa các lớp bông và thay bông mỗi hai ngày. Chỗ giấy đã mỏng đi và dễ rách, ông gia cố từ phía sau bằng giấy Nhật gần trong suốt, dán bằng keo loãng đến mức không để lại vệt bóng. Phần chữ, viết bằng mực gôm-sắt, vẫn rất bền — dù vài chữ trên ba trang đã phai, cần tô lại nhỏ, cẩn trọng, mà Hatem chỉ làm sau khi gia đình đồng ý và một học giả địa phương tư vấn về các chữ thiếu.

Phần đóng là giai đoạn cuối. Hatem sẽ khâu các trang lại với nhau bằng chỉ lanh, đúng như người đóng gốc thế kỷ XIX, và làm lại bìa bằng da từ một xưởng thuộc da ông đã dùng từ 1989. Nhưng ông sẽ không dùng da mới cho gáy. Ông đã để dành phần gáy gốc, bị hư do muối nhưng vẫn còn đọc được, và sẽ ghép vào trong phần đóng mới để bản thể cũ của cuốn sách vẫn hiện diện về mặt vật chất.

Gia đình sẽ nhận lại cuốn Quran tháng tới. Họ đã quyết nơi nó sẽ ngồi khi về nhà — không phải kệ thấp cũ, mà trên một giá tường trong căn phòng cụ cố từng đọc. Vết nước, Hatem nói với họ, sẽ nhạt đi chút trong mười năm nữa nhưng không bao giờ biến mất. Gia đình không có vẻ thất vọng. Họ nói cuốn sách giờ đã sống qua thêm một điều nữa.

options: A narrow lane behind the Zitouna Mosque in Tunis
Old paper, beeswax, and the glue Hatem mixes from rabbit-skin scraps and water
A winter storm pushed seawater into a coastal house; ~2 cm of water for half a day reached the low shelf; the lower third of every page was stained, swollen and sticking together

questions.prompt: What did Hatem honestly warn the family?
options: He could separate pages, dry them, repair the binding and rebuild the cover — but he could NOT make the water marks disappear
He could make it look new
By hand, using a thin blade and a brush of warm water applied one drop at a time

questions.prompt: How does he reinforce thinned paper?
options: From behind with a near-transparent Japanese tissue, glued with paste so dilute it leaves no shine

questions.prompt: What ink is the text written in, and how has it held up?
options: Iron-gall ink; held remarkably well, though a few words on three pages have faded

questions.prompt: Under what conditions did Hatem retouch faded words?
options: Only after the family agreed and a local scholar advised on the missing letters

questions.prompt: What will he use for the binding?
options: Linen thread (as the 19th-century binder did) and leather from a tannery he has used since 1989

questions.prompt: What will he do with the original spine?
options: Set it aside (salt-damaged but readable) and incorporate it into the new binding so the book's older self remains physically present

questions.prompt: Where will the family place it when it comes home?
options: Not back on the low shelf, but on a wall stand in the room where the great-grandfather used to read
A bookbinder restores a flood-damaged Quran without erasing the marks of what happened — the book is rebuilt around its memory of the flood, with the original spine kept in place.
Old books are not worth saving.
Water damage is always invisible after repair.

cloze.template:
The workshop sits in a narrow lane behind the Zitouna Mosque in Tunis. Hatem Mejri has worked there for forty years. He bound his first book — a school dictionary, {{b1}} for a teacher — when he was sixteen. His father bound books in the same room, and his grandfather before that. The room smells of old paper, beeswax, and the glue Hatem mixes from rabbit-skin {{b2}} and water.

In February of last year, a Quran arrived in a cardboard box. It had been kept for over a century in a small private library on the coast, inherited from a great-grandfather. A winter {{b3}} had pushed seawater into the house. The library had not flooded badly — perhaps two centimetres for half a day — but the Quran had been on a low shelf. The lower third of every page was stained, {{b4}}, and beginning to stick together. The family asked Hatem if anything could be done.

Hatem said yes, although he warned them honestly. He could separate the pages. He could dry them slowly, in a way that would not crack the ink. He could repair the binding and rebuild the cover. He could not, he said, make the water marks {{b5}}. Salt and time had already written themselves into the paper. The book would carry the memory of the flood. After a quiet half hour, the family asked him to begin.

The work has taken nine months so far. Hatem has separated each page by hand, using a thin {{b6}} and a brush of warm water applied one drop at a time. He has pressed pages between sheets of cotton and replaced the cotton every two days. Where the paper had thinned, he has reinforced it from behind with a near-transparent Japanese {{b7}}, glued on with a paste so {{b8}} it leaves no shine. The text, written in iron-gall ink, has held remarkably well — although a few words on three pages have faded and required tiny retouching, which Hatem did only after the family agreed and a local scholar advised on the missing letters.

The binding is the final stage. Hatem will sew the pages back together using {{b9}} thread, exactly as the original binder did in the nineteenth century, and rebuild the cover with leather from a tannery he has used since 1989. He will not, however, use new leather for the {{b10}}. He has set aside the original spine, salt-damaged but still readable, and will incorporate it into the new binding so that the book's older self remains physically present.

The family will collect the Quran next month. They have already decided where it will sit when it comes home — not back on the low shelf, but on a {{b11}} stand in the room where the great-grandfather used to read. The water marks, Hatem told them, will fade slightly over the next ten years but never vanish. The family did not seem disappointed. They said the book had now lived through one more thing.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Hatem rebuilt the book around the flood rather than pretending the flood never happened. Is there something in your own life that you have tried to repair by erasing the damage — and would it be different if you, like Hatem, kept the marks visible?
